topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white walls of the new office felt vast and unforgiving. Sarah clutched the strap of her worn messenger bag, the leather digging into her shoulder. Every time she walked past the open-plan workstations, she felt a sudden, intense scrutiny. People seemed to move with an easy confidence, their conversations flowing like a current she couldn't join. She tried to focus on the orientation packet in her hand, but the sheer volume of unfamiliar faces made her throat tighten. When her supervisor, Mark, finally approached, his smile was professional, but it didn't quite reach his eyes. "Welcome to Marketing," he said, his voice smooth as polished glass. Sarah managed a tight nod, her hands unconsciously twisting the fabric of her blouse. *I don't belong here,* she thought, the realization settling in her stomach like a cold stone.

[story 2]

The transfer meeting was a blur of jargon and shifting organizational charts. David sat in the back row, his knuckles white where he gripped his water bottle. He watched the other employees exchange knowing glances, a silent language he wasn't privy to. When his name was called for introductions in the new division, the spotlight felt blinding. He offered a practiced, small smile, but his gaze darted everywhere but at the speaker. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating. He noticed the way everyone seemed to know each other, the easy camaraderie that excluded him. Later, alone in the quiet breakroom, he stared at his reflection in the stainless steel appliance, noticing the tremor in his hands. He just wanted the familiar comfort of his old desk, the predictable rhythm of his tasks.

[story 3]

Elara spent the first week navigating the labyrinthine corridors of the R&D wing. The equipment hummed with a high-pitched energy that made her ears ring. She felt perpetually out of sync, like a badly tuned instrument. Every time a colleague asked for input, Elara found herself stammering, her carefully rehearsed thoughts dissolving into nervous whispers. She avoided eye contact, focusing instead on the meticulous arrangement of components on the bench before her. One afternoon, while trying to explain a procedural detail, her voice cracked midway through a sentence. A silence followed, heavy and judgmental. Elara quickly averted her gaze, feeling a flush creep up her neck, wishing the floor would simply swallow her whole.
